lesson.title: Working with text
masteryOutcomes: Define a "data type" as a category of data that determines what you can do with it.
Create a string variable by enclosing text in single (') or double (") quotes.
Combine two strings using the + operator (concatenation).
Predict the output when printing string variables.

concept: Strings: the data type for text — creating them with quotes, combining them with +, and predicting exactly what prints
prerequisites: Introduction to variables: assigning a value to a name
print() basics: displaying a value on the screen

explanations.text: Computers sort information into kinds, like drawers: numbers in one, text in another. The kind is called a data type, and it decides what you're allowed to do — you can do math with numbers, and you can glue text together. Text in Python is called a string: any characters wrapped in quotes, like "hello" or 'hello'. And + glues strings: "hip" + "po" makes "hippo".

A data type is a category of data that determines what you can do with it — the same + means math for numbers but gluing for text. Text values are strings, created by wrapping characters in matching quotes: name = "Ava" and name = 'Ava' make exactly the same string. + between two strings is called concatenation: it joins them exactly, adding nothing — not even a space. And printing a string variable shows its contents without the quotes, so after greeting = "Hi " + name, print(greeting) shows Hi Ava.

The type of a value decides how operators behave: + concatenates two strings but adds two numbers, which is why "5" + "5" is "55" while 5 + 5 is 10. Quotes are delimiters, not part of the value — single and double quotes produce identical strings ('Ava' == "Ava" is True), and you pick whichever avoids clashing with quotes inside the text, like "It's fine". Concatenation builds a new string character for character, so every space you want in the output must live inside one of the strings. Two failure modes to know: text without quotes (name = Ava) makes Python look up a variable called Ava and raise a NameError, and "age is " + 25 raises a TypeError because + won't mix strings and numbers.

hintLadder.text: How does Python tell text apart from code? Something has to wrap the text on both sides.
Assign quoted text to a name, like word = "hello" — the quotes must match on both ends.
To combine, put + between two strings — and remember + adds nothing extra, not even a space.
Before running, read your expression left to right and write down the exact characters it builds — including every space.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes text without quotes, e.g. name = John
misconceptions.correction: Without quotes, Python treats John as a variable name to look up and raises a NameError. Wrap the text in quotes: name = "John".
The opening and closing quotes must match — both single or both double. name = 'John' and name = "John" both work; a mismatched pair is a syntax error.
Concatenation is exact: it produces HelloWorld. Any space must be inside a string — "Hello" + " " + "World" or "Hello " + "World".

misconceptions.trigger: Concatenates a string with a number, e.g. "age is " + 25
misconceptions.correction: + needs strings on both sides, so this raises a TypeError. Convert first — "age is " + str(25) — or use a comma inside print: print("age is", 25).

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks print(greeting) will show the word greeting, or show the value with quotes around it
misconceptions.correction: print shows the value the variable holds, not the variable's name and not the quotes. If greeting = "Hi Ava", then print(greeting) shows exactly: Hi Ava.

misconceptions.trigger: Confidently claims single and double quotes create different kinds of strings
misconceptions.correction: They are identical — 'Ava' == "Ava" is True. The choice is convenience: use double quotes when the text contains an apostrophe, like "It's fine".
Both values are strings, so + concatenates: the result is "55". This is data types in action — the type decides what + does. (Numbers get their own lesson next.)

checks.prompt: Create two string variables, firstName and lastName, and use + to print the full name with exactly one space between the parts.
acceptanceCriteria: Both variables are assigned quoted strings
Uses + to concatenate, including an explicit " " for the space
Prints the full name with exactly one space between first and last name
checks.passFeedback: That's real string-building — you controlled every character, space included.
checks.failFeedback: Check the quotes on both variables, and remember + adds nothing by itself — the space has to be a string like " " in the middle.

checks.prompt:
Predict before running — what exactly does this show?
s = "5" + "5"
print(s)
acceptanceCriteria: Explains that both values are strings, so + concatenates instead of adding
Connects it to the idea that the data type determines what an operation does
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — same + symbol, different type, different behavior. That's what a data type means.
checks.failFeedback: Look at the quotes: "5" is text, not a number. + between two strings glues them, so you get 55 — the type decides what + does.

practice.prompt: Create a variable animal with any animal name and a variable sound with the noise it makes. Using + only, print a full sentence like: The cat says meow — and mind the spaces.
acceptanceCriteria: Builds the sentence with + including explicit spaces
The printed sentence reads correctly with single spaces between words

practice.prompt: In your own words: what is a data type, and why does Python care whether a value is text or a number?
rubric: Defines a data type as a category of data that determines what you can do with it
Gives an example of type-dependent behavior, such as + joining strings but adding numbers, or math being impossible on text

options: An error, because the quote styles don't match
practice.explanation: Single and double quotes make the same string type, so mixing styles across two separate strings is fine. The space lives inside 'Hello ', and + joins exactly — output: Hello world.

pedagogyRules: Ask for a prediction of the exact printed output before every run, then compare character by character — spaces count.
If the student asks a jump-ahead question (f-strings, .upper(), len()), answer in one sentence, note that richer text tools arrive in Week 2, and return to quotes and +.
If the student says they already know strings, verify lightly: ask them to predict s = "5" + "5" — a correct answer with the type-based reason means skip straight to the full-name check.
If the student is lost, shrink the task: create one quoted variable and print it; only after that works, introduce + with two pieces.
When the student is wrong but confident (e.g. insists "5" + "5" is 10), have them run it, then anchor the surprise to the rule: the data type decides what + does.
If the student wants to skip, allow it after they pass the full-name check on the first try; otherwise fix one misconception first.

When a NameError or TypeError frustrates the student, acknowledge the feeling in one line, then translate the error into plain words and point at the single-character fix (a missing quote or a str()).
If asked why this matters, answer in one line — most real data is text (names, emails, messages), and building sentences from pieces happens in nearly every program — then continue.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Read the real error. NameError after unquoted text means missing quotes; TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str means a number sits next to +. Name the actual cause from the message — never invent another reason.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor validates output that doesn't match
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when the printed characters match exactly. AdaLovelace fails a task that requires Ada Lovelace — if spacing differs, say so and point at the missing " ".

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — solution dumped too fast
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Give one hint from the ladder at a time and let the student run code between hints; never write the full assignment-plus-print solution for them.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Emotional attunement miss on first error messages
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: A first NameError or TypeError can rattle a beginner. Acknowledge the feeling before explaining, then show that the fix is one small edit — a quote or a str().

chunks.text: A data type is a category of data that determines what you can do with it. Strings are the data type for text. The type decides how operators behave: + joins two strings but adds two numbers, so "5" + "5" is "55" while 5 + 5 is 10.

A string is created by enclosing text in matching single or double quotes: name = 'Ava' and name = "Ava" are identical. The quotes are delimiters, not part of the value. Text without quotes, like name = Ava, makes Python look for a variable called Ava and raise a NameError.

The + operator concatenates strings: it joins them exactly, adding nothing — not even a space. "Ada" + "Lovelace" is "AdaLovelace"; to get a space, include one in a string: "Ada" + " " + "Lovelace". Concatenating a string with a number raises a TypeError; fix it with str(), e.g. "age is " + str(25).

print shows a variable's value, not its name and not the quotes. Worked example: first = "Ada", last = 'Lovelace', full = first + " " + last, print(full) — the output is exactly: Ada Lovelace. Predicting output means tracing the exact characters the expression builds, spaces included.

celebration.headline: Text is officially yours.
celebration.recap: You defined what a data type is, created string variables with single and double quotes, glued strings with + (spaces included, on purpose), and predicted printed output exactly.
celebration.unlocked: Next: working with numbers — ints, floats, and why 5 is different from "5".

summaryBullets: You can now define a "data type" as a category of data that determines what you can do with it.
You can now create a string variable by enclosing text in single (') or double (") quotes.
You can now combine two strings using the + operator (concatenation).
You can now predict the output when printing string variables.
